summary: Stancil argues that the “modern data stack” fad died but is now partially resurfacing through enterprise vendors like SAP and Databricks, not as warehouse‑native apps but as shared data formats and tighter platform integrations. He’s skeptical that this will fully solve messy enterprise data, but sees a plausible path where applications and analytics tools connect through common storage frameworks rather than rebuilding the stack. Alongside that, he reflects on how data systems remain chaotic in practice and riffs on parallel stories about institutional messiness and financial oddities.
money_quotes: The whole modern data stack movement is dead, cringe and out of fashion—replaced, obviously, with AI.
You can build a trailer on a modular foundation, but you can’t build a mansion, and enterprises like their mansions.
If the world gradually coalesces around common data storage frameworks, people might not rebuild applications on top of databases but on top of data formats.
We all laugh at FTX for their terrible balance sheet of one-off math and “hidden, poorly labeled accounts,” but we are all FTX; it’s just a matter of degree.
Trillion-dollar transformations start whizbang brands on Twitter, but end with the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).
key_insight: The modern data stack failed as a warehouse‑native revolution, but a quieter revival may emerge through shared data formats and vendor integrations—without fixing the underlying messiness of enterprise data.
